headline: GRAINS SIDEWAYS AS METALS CRATER
subheadline: Corn and beans drift while gold's worst day in months signals broader risk-off mood.
lead: Grain futures barely budged overnight while precious metals got hammered — gold down 5.2% and silver off 8.4% in their worst session since late 2024. The divergence tells the story: ag fundamentals are holding steady while financial markets recalibrate risk. With planting decisions looming and USDA's Prospective Plantings report three weeks out, this sideways action might be the calm before the storm.
teaser: Metals crater while grains hold — what the divergence means for your spring decisions.

one_number.context: New-crop corn futures gained a penny while nearby stayed flat — a subtle but important signal that the market sees tighter supplies ahead. This 25¢ premium over cash corn reflects growing confidence in demand destruction from higher prices. That spread could be your friend if you're willing to price some 2026 production now.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Corn barely flinched while December futures crept higher — a sign the market's looking past current abundance toward tighter new-crop scenarios. Soybeans split the difference with nearby beans up a penny and November down the same amount. The real story is what didn't happen: grains ignored the broader risk-off mood that crushed metals. Wheat dropped 2¼¢ on continued Black Sea competition, but even that's minor compared to the chaos elsewhere. Oats bucked the trend with a 1.1% rally on short covering.
sections.bottom_line: Grains showing remarkable stability while everything else wobbles.
sections.farmer_action: Use this stability to assess spring planting plans — volatility is coming with USDA reports.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Live cattle edged higher while Class III milk took a 2.8% hit — the biggest move in the livestock complex today. Feed costs remain supportive with corn steady and soybean meal up 0.4%, giving cattle feeders some breathing room. Lean hogs gained modestly despite export concerns lingering from recent trade tensions. The milk collapse reflects seasonal patterns and ongoing margin pressure for dairy producers. Feeder cattle dipped slightly as ranchers weigh spring grass conditions against feed costs.
sections.bottom_line: Mixed signals but feed costs staying manageable for now.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor milk futures for potential forward pricing opportunities if you're in dairy.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Crude oil surged 2.9% to $74.80 on fresh geopolitical tensions, while natural gas held steady around $3.04. This oil rally translates directly to higher diesel and fertilizer costs just as spring applications begin across the Corn Belt. The timing couldn't be worse for input planning — every $10 move in crude adds roughly 25¢ to diesel and pressures nitrogen prices. Natural gas stability provides some relief for fertilizer producers, but crude's strength is the dominant factor for field operations costs.
sections.bottom_line: Oil surge means higher fieldwork costs heading into spring.
sections.farmer_action: Consider topping off diesel storage now before further crude oil strength hits pump prices.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar strengthened while stocks fell, but the real shock was precious metals' collapse — gold's 5.2% drop and silver's 8.4% plunge marked their worst session in months. This wasn't agriculture-driven but rather a broad unwinding of inflation hedges as markets reassess Fed policy. The stronger dollar theoretically pressures grain exports, but today's ag market ignored that signal completely. S&P 500 fell 0.9% as risk appetite soured, yet grains held their ground remarkably well.
sections.bottom_line: Financial markets in turmoil but grains march to their own drum.
sections.farmer_action: No action needed — grain fundamentals trumping macro noise for now.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Grains Ignored the Metal Meltdown
the_more_you_know.body: When gold and silver crater while grains hold steady, it usually signals that agricultural fundamentals are strong enough to overcome macro headwinds. Precious metals are pure financial plays — they move on Fed policy, inflation expectations, and risk sentiment. Grains have real supply and demand drivers that can insulate them from broader market chaos. Today's divergence suggests the ag market sees through current volatility to underlying tight supply scenarios. That independence is bullish for grain prices medium-term.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales report — watch for corn and soybean demand signals.
Crude oil inventory data — further strength could pressure input costs.
Weather models for early planting conditions across Iowa and Illinois.
USDA Prospective Plantings report — the biggest market mover before planting season.

daily_quote.text: To own a bit of ground, to scratch it with a hoe, to plant seeds, and watch the renewal of life — this is the commonest delight of the race.

source_summary: CME Group · USDA · EIA · Federal Reserve
